responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

The Bulletin of the Hampstead Clinic—Announcement

The staff of the Hampstead Child-Therapy Clinic are happy to announce the appearance in 1978 of the Bulletin of the Hampstead Clinic. The Clinic was founded by Anna Freud over a quarter of a century ago and is recognized as the foremost child psychoanalytic training and research centre in the world. A vast number of hitherto unpublished clinical reports and other material of psychoanalytic interest has been accumulated and now appears for the first time.

The new Bulletin will give an ongoing picture of all aspects of the past, present and future work of the Clinic, and will provide valuable source material for all workers in the field of psychoanalytic therapy and in allied professions. Special emphasis will be placed on clinical topics and on the integration of clinical observations with psychoanalytic theory; discussions with Anna Freud and other senior staff members will be included and the clinical application of such instruments as the Diagnostic Profile and the Hampstead Index will be documented. Volume 1 Part 1 is scheduled for early 1978, and Volume 1 will include papers and reports on a variety of topics, including original contributions by Anna Freud.

The Bulletin of the Hampstead Clinic will appear four times a year. The price for the yearly volume of four issues is US $20.00 (or £10.00 for U.K. addresses only). Subscriptions should be sent (with cheque or money order) to: The Bulletin of the Hampstead Clinic
21 Maresfield Gardens
London NW3 5SU

responseSet.title: Therapeutic Dilemmas Relevant to the Lifting of Repression
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Therapeutic Dilemmas Relevant to the Lifting of Repression

Paul Myerson

Paul Myerson

Paul G. Myerson
Department of Psychiatry Tufts University School of Medicine
171 Harrison Avenue
Boston, Mass. 02111

Paul Myerson  

The concept of repression was introduced at the inception of psychoanalysis and the lifting of repression remains the major aim of the psychoanalytic method. The primary purpose of the analyst is to make it possible for the patient to experience, recall and tolerate the feelings, fantasies and memories he has up to this point repressed. How this occurs and how it is 'curative' has been and continues to be the subject of considerable discussion, for example by Strachey (1934), Yorke (1965), Bird (1972), Klauber (1972), Rosenfeld (1972), Compton (1975) and Southwood (1975).

Repression proper is first manifest at the time of the childhood neurosis. It helps to mitigate the child's anxiety engendered by the expression of intense libidinal and aggressive feelings towards those persons upon whom he is dependent. Repression, that is, the turning away of attention from these strong feelings, allows the child to maintain meaningful relationships with his parents and his siblings. However, as is the case with all defensive measures, a price is paid for this. Even though the process of repression occurs at a period when there has been considerable maturation of the cognitive functions, the turning away of attention from crucial inner experiences creates what might be considered a maturational lag. The maturational lag is particularly apparent in those situations later on in life which are likely to evoke intense libidinal and aggressive reactions.

In ordinary situations, that is, in other than the psychoanalytic one, the maturational lag associated with the process of repression limits the individual's capacity, when involved in libidinal and aggressive interchanges, to recognize adequately the intentions of other people. Because of the anxiety and inhibition which continues to be mobilized in sexual or aggressive situations over a period of years, there is an impairment in the capacity to discriminate between those actions and statements from other people which are intended to be helpful and those with a more malevolent intent, e.g. between an attempt at consolation and an intent at seduction. In addition, the maturational lag is associated with a limited integration of libidinal and aggressive feelings into the self, thereby restricting the use of feelings1 as a means for placing transactions of this character into the appropriate context. Finally, the maturational lag is associated with a limited acceptance of responsibility for libidinal and aggressive feelings, perpetuating in various ways a dependency upon other individuals.

responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Models of Madness, Models of Medicine

By Miriam Sigler and Humphrey Osmond. New York and London: Macmillan. 1974. Pp. 287.

Peter Shoenberg

Peter Shoenberg

Peter Shoenberg  

This book sets out to examine the various models of mental illness currently obtaining in Western psychiatry. The authors' aim as stated is to rationalize some of the confusion that arises in psychiatric discussions. They divide the psychiatric models into two groups: first, those groups which do not offer global theories of the mind, which they call the 'discontinuous models', namely the medical, the moral and the impairment models, and secondly, those models which they regard as offering global theories of the mind, which they describe as the 'continuous models', namely the psychoanalytic, the psychedelic, the conspiratorial, the social and the family interaction models. In particular they argue that the continuous models of mental illness have tended to eschew the issue of sickness in the patient. They single out the social, the psychedelic and conspiratorial models on this count. There is an interesting parallel drawn between the current idealization of the schizophrenic experience as seen in the psychedelic model and the past romanticization of a medical condition, tuberculosis, before the time when this latter illness was amenable to successful treatment.

The authors' chief concern is to establish the validity and efficaciousness of the medical model within psychiatry. In examining this model, while they explore the roles and attitudes adopted by the doctor and patient in their relationship to each other, they do not pursue to any great extent the difference between this model's doctor–patient relationship and the relationship obtaining in other models. This is an issue which has been of great interest to some structural anthropologists who have tried to show the ways in which changes in this two-fold relationship have a bearing on the three-fold relationship of the doctor and patient and the concept of illness. The main focus of the authors is on the incapacity of the continuous models to explain and cope with the major psychoses. In particular they point out how certain models have led to a 'flight from the patient'. The authors' point, that our abandonment of the medical model in current psychiatry may lead to an eventual flight from the severely ill patient, is well stated at a time when in some countries many severely ill mental patients are being discharged from their asylums into a relatively indifferent society.

responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

A Phase of Depression in a Six-Months-Old Boy

J. Wisdom

J. Wisdom

J. O. Wisdom
Department of Philosophy, York University
4700 Keele Street
Downsview, Ontario M3J 1P3

J. Wisdom  

The following sequence of observations concerns the eldest of a family, a little boy. The details were noted by the father at the time. Their significance lies on their bearing on hypotheses about loss, depression, working through and integration; and in addition on the relationship between theory and observation.

The first six months were relatively uneventful. Then for a few (about three or four) days the little boy's cry carried a dismal note which, if it occurred in an adult, would instantly be put down to grief or depression. Moreover, he could not stay even in the presence of his mother and father without crying to be picked up. He had to be held or carried. When he was put down after being carried about for a while, this crying would begin again at once.

Allow me to sketch in the background. The family had just gone on holiday, but without the third person the little boy was accustomed to, namely the family nanny, who was a nannie in the best old-fashioned sense. She was away elsewhere. The family arrived at the little boy's grandmother's, where he was looked after both by his mother and his granny. He got on very well with his granny and got to know her in some degree over the course of a week. The family then left for a cottage. (To interpolate a conjecture at this point, one might suppose that he felt he had lost one person, had found another, and though he knew her less well and she mattered less to him, he had lost her too, and might well be wondering in the infantile equivalent to an adult doubt, 'Whom am I going to lose next?') In fact, he clung to his mother in a new way (afraid that perhaps he would lose her next?). And this was also the case with his father, though more marked with his mother. This new dependence on his parents began almost at once on leaving his grandmother's, and the phase of what seemed to be marked depression developed nearly a fortnight later. In view of the circumstances, the father remarked that he had expected some depressive disturbance but was taken by surprise at the stark naked appearance of depression.

responseSet.title: A Model of Archaic Thinking Based on Two Formulations of Freud
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

A Model of Archaic Thinking Based on Two Formulations of Freud

Andrew Peto

Andrew Peto

Andrew Peto
1225 Park Avenue
New York, N.Y. 10028

Andrew Peto  

SUMMARY

I have endeavoured to demonstrate in this paper that the synthetic function of the ego as conceived by Nunberg, who included the concept of a variety of defence mechanisms, consists in two basic autonomous ego functions: fragmenting negative hallucination and fusing positive hallucination. Under normal conditions they operate silently; however, under pathological conditions in severe borderlines and in psychotics and deep regressions in the transference, they may become manifest. I have suggested a completed primary model of thinking, combining Freud's model of 1900 with his suggestion in 1915. This model consists in the harmonious or disharmonious oscillations of the two above quoted operations. Beyond the thought model I have attempted to relate them to clinical phenomena in general. Positive hallucination causes the fusing and merging of images and mental representations in general. Negative hallucination, through manifesting itself in the clinical phenomena of fragmentation, relates to the clinically observable signs of hiatuses and disintegrations in thinking and the haphazard disconnexions in the emergence of mental representations and of the accompanying affects and feeling moods.

Finally, I have referred briefly to the significance of these assumptions for transference and countertransference in the therapeutic situation.

responseSet.title: Learning Disabilities as Related to a Special Form of Mothering
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Learning Disabilities as Related to a Special Form of Mothering

Ben Rubenstein and Morton Levitt

Morton Levitt

Ben Rubenstein
BRATTLEBORO VT.
Morton Levitt
Office of the Dean University of California School of Medicine
Davis, Calif. 95616

Morton Levitt  

Two events which have occurred in recent years illuminate the relationship between psycho-analytic theory and the development of cognitive skills. First, a lengthy study by Christopher Jencks and his research group revealed two surprising conclusions: (1) that schools merely solidify and certify learning inequalities that children bring with them, and (2) that the effects of school on the development of cognitive skills depends on a single factor, that is, the characteristics—called the idiosyncratic traits—of the children entering school (The New York Times, 28 September 1972).

Secondly, Burton L. White, director of the preschool project at the Harvard Graduate School of Education, after having reviewed the developmental records of hundreds of children, has stated that 'the difference between successful and unsuccessful children in relation to learning is rooted in their early handling' (The New York Times, 21 October 1973). White adds that the actions of the mother are 'the most powerful, formative factors in the development of a preschool child'.

Society's increased interest in learning difficulties has led us to rethink our clinical experiences with children with cognitive problems.1 This was an arena in which we have worked on and off for the past 15 years. At first attracted by the seeming powerlessness of young individuals to master educational tasks, we identified the condition as 'learning impotence' (Rubenstein et al., 1959) and described it in general terms at the American Psychoanalytic Association meeting in 1958 and at the American Orthopsychiatric meeting a year later. A few years after that, we were drawn to the preliminary concept of ego distortion which we presented to the Paris Psychoanalytic Society in 1961 (Rubenstein et al., 1961). We have continued to mull over this exotic problem, and this paper brings our thinking up to date. We still hold to the ego distortion construct, but we feel that we are now able to delineate more clearly what we believe to be its generic origin.

responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Discussion of the Paper by E. R. Shapiro, R. L. Shapiro, J. Zinner and D. A. Berkowitz on 'The Borderline Ego and the Working Alliance: Indications for Family and Individual Treatment in Adolescence'

Dan Buie

Dan Buie

Dan H. Buie, Jr.
New England Medical Center Hospital
171 Harrison Avenue
Boston, Mass. 02111

Dan Buie  

This paper by E. R. Shapiro et al. (this issue) reports another segment of the authors' excellent clinical studies of disturbed adolescents and their families. I discern only one difficulty, and that is their use of the concept of splitting. A brief recapitulation of their thesis will prepare the way for clarifying my reservations.

The thesis of the paper is that the borderline adolescent is compelled by a variety of forces (striving for autonomy is one) towards separation from his family. This separation intensifies life and death dependency needs, and it exacerbates tension over similar dependency issues in the parents. The resulting distress is more than any of the troubled family can tolerate; they revert as a group to pervasive use of certain defences which are developed at an earlier stage in their life cycles. The authors identify these defences as splitting, projective identification and, by implication, introjection. The family employs these defences in such a way as to fixate the adolescent's pathological state; his reaction to the parents' use of projective identification is especially pernicious.

The borderline adolescent is very often intensely negativistic in impulses, feelings, and ideation towards his parents, the therapist and himself. Non-specific ego weakness and his ready resort to projective identification frequently make it difficult for him to perceive his therapist as really helpful. The working alliance is especially vulnerable to being capsized by hostilities emanating from family interactions. The authors therefore hold that the therapist should work both with the family as a group and with the adolescent individually. When the therapist clarifies and corrects pathological family interactions, his patient has less trouble to see him as a helpful person who is different from pathological introjects. The leaky working alliance can be plugged up in this way, and therapy can move ahead more securely.

The clinical data and thesis of the authors seem quite solid, with one exception: their use of the concept of splitting, as it has been defined by Kernberg (1966), (1967). Here there are three problems. (1) They invoke the concept unnecessarily and in the absence of substantiating clinical data. (2) They misapply the concept in their attempt to explain how the parents derive their negative images of the adolescent as 'ruthlessly independent' or 'ravenously demanding and totally dependent'. (3) They stretch the concept of splitting significantly beyond its limits in trying to force it to encompass the conflict between autonomy and dependency, which they see as the heart of the problem for the borderline adolescent and his family. Each of these problems will be discussed.

responseSet.title: On the Formation of Schizophrenic Symptoms
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

On the Formation of Schizophrenic Symptoms

Ping-Nie Pao

Ping-Nie Pao

Ping-Nie Pao
Chestnut Lodge
Rockville
Maryland 20850

Ping-Nie Pao  

SUMMARY

In the above, I have considered symptomformation in schizophrenia mainly in the light of Freud's new theory of anxiety (1926), (1933) and Hartmann's concept of integrative ego functions leading to an adaptation to inner and external reality (see also Arlow & Brenner, 1964), (1969). A clinical case has been presented to illustrate that five steps can be distinguished in symptom formation: (1) the resurfacing of conflicts; (2) an experience of 'organismic panic'; (3) a disturbance in ego functioning and a loss of the continuous sense of self; (4) thereupon the integrative function of the ego orders and modulates the mental content, so as to find a best possible solution to alleviate the experience of organismic panic; and (5) the symptom formation and an altered sense of self become crystallized in the wake of the best possible solution.

The catatonic, the paranoid, the hebephrenic or the simple symptomatology may be viewed as the best possible solution attained by a patient at a given time. Although some patients show a predilection to one or another type of symptomatology, each of such symptomatologies is expected to be encountered in any given patient in the course of long-term treatment to meet the requirement of a best possible solution.

Although in the illustrative case the anxietyprovoking incident was discreet and readily identifiable, and the anxiety reaction was intense and dramatic, the five steps in symptom formation in schizophrenia are not restricted to these kinds of discreet situations. The same observation can be

399

made in cases where the schizophrenic symptoms seem to come on stealthily and very gradually. In the latter type of cases, the five steps can be reconstructed in the course of treatment and in addition can be discerned in situ in the therapeutic setting.

In reaching for a best possible solution, or in the symptom formation, the self has undergone reorganization as well. I have suggested that the degree of consolidation of the reorganized self may be used to delineate the acute from the subacute phase of schizophrenic illness. I have attempted to demonstrate that the concept of reorganization of the self is an important one, for it helps to shape our strategy in the treatment of schizophrenic patients.

responseSet.title: On Freud's Theory of Schizophrenia
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

On Freud's Theory of Schizophrenia

Thomas Freeman

Thomas Freeman

Thomas Freeman
DUNDEE
Longforgan
Perthshire
Scotand

Thomas Freeman  

For many years now there has been a continuing and sustained criticism of Freud's (1911) hypothesis that the basic disorder in schizophrenia consists in the patient's inability to maintain the libidinal cathexis of objects. The fact that patients suffering from the two principal types of schizophrenia present signs of real and fantasy object relationships has been taken as evidence that the illness cannot be based on a decathexis of object representations. This contradiction is easily dispelled if account is taken of the real likelihood that these object relationships, which are mostly of a pathological kind, represent a spontaneous tendency towards recovery. They are therefore secondary to the primary disorder and a reaction to it.

In the original theory Freud (1911) described the flight of the libidinal cathexis from the love object in schizophrenia as a form of repression. After the introduction of the structural theory (Freud, 1923) he dropped the term 'repression' when describing the metapsychology of schizophrenia (Freud, 1924) but adhered to his earlier view that the psychosis is initiated by a withdrawal of object cathexis in the face of a danger situation. The course of the illness and its clinical characteristics depend on the balance which is struck between the forces promoting the decathexis of objects and the pressure exerted by the libidinal wishes seeking to recathect the objects. This balance varies between patients and within the one patient at different stages of the illness, so accounting for the different clinical types and for the changing symptomatology in the same patient. It follows from the classical theory (Fenichel, 1945) that the symptomatology characteristic of schizophrenia must have different sources of origin and must be formed by different mechanisms.

I

At the onset of a schizophrenia or during its course, it is commonplace to observe a primitive identification with the object (Nunberg, 1921); (Katan 1950); (Reich, 1936). In chronic cases relating at this level may be the predominant form of object relationship (Freeman, Cameron & McGhie, 1958). Where these phenomena occur, at onset or in chronicity, there is a widespread dissolution of psychic structure. Objects of significance to the patient lose their autonomy and are condensed with other representations as well as with the self. All these observations suggest that the inability to maintain object cathexis, whether regarded as a defensive measure or as a functional failure of unknown cause (A. Freud, personal communication, 1976) leads to an identification with the object. A further effect of the decathexis is a hypercathexis of the self—a pathological narcissism which finds expression in the grandiosity and ego-centrism of the schizophrenic patient.

responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Depersonalization: A Self-Relations Perspective

Allen Frances , Michael Sacks and Michael Aronoff

Michael Aronoff

Allen Frances
Department of Psychiatry Cornell University Medical Center
525 East 68th Street
New York, N.Y. 10021
Michael Sacks
Michael S. Aronoff
NEW YORK

Michael Aronoff  

SUMMARY

This paper explores personalization and depersonalization with special emphasis on the ego function of self-constancy. We regard the self as an intrapsychic structure and the ego as a group of functions that differentiates and integrates the self and, amongst many other things, maintains an affective experience of 'me-ness'. Defects in self-object differentiation, in self-constancy and in object constancy result in psychotic, borderline and narcissistic vulnerabilities to depersonalization. Sudden changes in the self representations not conforming to the average expectable self challenge self-constancy and may result in depersonalization. Depersonalization may at times be a signal affect denoting challenge to self-constancy from ego fixation or regression or from the presence of previously unavailable self- or self-object representations. It may in another model, at times, be a defensive affect protecting the ego from painful stimuli arising within the self-structure.

responseSet.title: New Theories: Their Influence and Effect on Psychoanalytic Technique
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

New Theories: Their Influence and Effect on Psychoanalytic Technique

A. Bahia

A. Bahia

A. B. Bahia
c/o Rosa Beatriz Pontes de Miranda
Rua Assis Brasil, 118, ap. 101
ZC-07 - Copacabana
20.000 Rio de Janeiro, Brazil

A. Bahia  

SUMMARY

This paper aims at studying ideas which have contributed to the development of psychoanalytical technique, from Freud to our present time, with special attention to Bion's findings.

Three aspects of trends which seem to be basic to psychoanalytical technique at the moment are emphasized: (1) the 'saturation' of the mind by memory and desire (preconceptions, distortions,

361

anticipations); (2) inability to cope with frustration and incapacity for thinking; and (3) the simultaneity of the pleasure principle with the reality principle.

These aspects stem from three original sources: (1) the significance of 'remembering' and of 'forgetting' according to Freud; (2) the reappraisal of the oedipal conflict by Melanie Klein; and (3) Freud's studies on 'Loss of Reality in Neurosis and Psychosis' (1924b) and on 'The Two Principles of Mental Functioning' (Freud, 1911).

This conceptual revision leads to consequences of extraordinary practical importance:

1. Once the mind is released from the distortions, falsifications and subterfuges by which the memory tries to 'explain' the past and the desire seeks to 'anticipate' the future; once the psyche faces the impossibility of evading frustration through omnipotence; and as the simultaneity of the pleasure principle and the reality principle is understood, both analyst and analysand can concentrate with special application on the 'here and now' situation, and face more unequivocally the need to be truthful.

2. To be truthful 'here and now' implies the recognition that not all events in the analytical situation are necessarily repetitive, although they include repetitions. If the theory of 'compulsion to repetition' is looked at critically, quite the opposite can be verified; that is to say, the analytical process evolves constantly towards the new, thus discarding schematic reductionism to childhood.

3. From observation of facts, countertransference can be said to be the result of successful projective identifications by the patient, which implies that it is questionable as a working-tool.

[[RunningHead]]

Clinical Essay Prize

Herbert Rosenfeld M.D.

Herbert Rosenfeld

Herbert Rosenfeld, M.D., Chairman
Scientific Committee, Institute of Psycho-Analysis
63 New Cavendish Street, London W.1.

Herbert Rosenfeld  

The Clinical Essay Prize was founded in 1932 under a Trust Deed which laid down the amount of the award, the requirements of the essay, the procedure for entering the competition, and the rules governing the publication of prize essays.

All conditions were reviewed by the Trustees and certain changes approved which took effect from 1970. The closing date for the next competition is 31 March 1978.

The conditions currently governing the competition are as follows:

A prize of £75 is offered, and the closing date for applications is 31 March 1978.

REQUIREMENTS FOR THE ESSAY

The essay shall consist of a clinical record of a case treated by psychoanalysis. It should illustrate clearly the events and changes in the mental life of the patient and their relation to external environment. In awarding the prize, the judges will pay attention to acuity of observation and the clarity with which the facts are stated. If the writer wishes to draw theoretical conclusions, he must bear in mind the necessity of making the evidence for such conclusions carry conviction.

It is recommended that the length of the essay should not exceed 20, 000 words.

The essay shall not have been published in any book, journal, or other form of publication and shall not have been read to or have formed the subject of discussion at any formally constituted meeting of psychoanalysts.

DATE OF SENDING IN ESSAYS: LANGUAGE, FORMAT, ETC.

The prize is awarded every second year and essays must reach the Institute on or before 31 March in the competition year. They must be in the English language, in typescript on quarto paper with ample left-hand margin. They must be in triplicate and be sent to the Chairman of the Scientific Committee of the Institute. All copies of essays submitted become ipso facto the property of the Institute (or its successor) while it has the appointment of the Trustees for the Prize Fund.

NO AWARD

If no essay of merit worthy of a prize is submitted in any competition year, no award shall be made in that year.

[[RunningHead]]

Hamlet's Enemy: Madness and Myth in 'Hamlet'

By Theodore Lidz. London: Vision. 1975. Pp. 285.

John Padel

John Padel

John Padel  

A writer with two or more skills or strong interests often brings them together to find that they have thrown light upon, even fructified, each other. If they have done so for him, they may for others; hence Hamlet's Enemy. Theodore Lidz's mind ranges widely, as befits a famous practitioner and Yale Professor of Psychiatry, and he has at least two special interests beside his profession: he has studied Hamlet and the literature about it for 25 years; he has also studied mythology and with others has written a paper on Cargo Cultism in Melanesian Society. He is a psychoanalyst with a special interest in schizophrenias. The theme common to these apparently diverse passions is of course the family; 'The Family: The Source of Human Resources' is the title of one of his essays: in 1963 he published a 3-essay book called 'The Family and Human Adaptation'.

On 19 September 1975, in the Times Literary Supplement, Dr J. K. Wing reviewed Lidz's last book, The Origin and Treatment of Schizophrenic Disorders. The review evoked a longish letter by Lidz (21 November) and that a shorter reply from Wing (5 December). From stalls or pit it was clear that each maintained that the other was excluding an important middle. Dr Wing claimed that Dr Lidz wrote as if no child brought up in a family belonging to one or two types could fail to be schizophrenic and as if no schizophrenia could occur except in such a family; Dr Lidz in effect replied that Wing wrote as if the particular family structure (personalities and their interaction)

responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Theme and Variation in the Development of the First Transitional Object

Fred Busch and Judith McKnight

Judith McKnight

Fred Busch
410 Orchard Hills Drive
Ann Arbor, Mich. 48104
Judith McKnight
ANN ARBOR

Judith McKnight  

SUMMARY

In our research we have been attempting to elucidate the phenomenon we have labelled the 'first transitional object'. In this paper we have focused primarily on some dimensions along which the child can vary in his use of the first transitional object. We have pointed to the fact that some infants first come in contact with the 'blanket' at the age of three months, and this seems to be a critical time period for regular contact with the first transitional object. While there are variations in the child's attachment to the first transitional object from the very beginning, age two appears to be a crucial time when

485

the intensity of the child's attachment to the 'blanket' becomes established. Most children seem to give up the first transitional object between five and six years of age, and an example of the disruption of this process was presented. An attempt was made to put these findings within a developmental context, while other variables which affect the fate of the first transitional object were also explored.

responseSet.title: The Morning Ruminative State—The Flash Phenomenon
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

The Morning Ruminative State—The Flash Phenomenon

John Frosch

John Frosch

John Frosch
Department of Psychiatry, Brookdale Hospital Medical Center
Linden Blvd & Rockway Parkway
Brooklyn, New York 11212

John Frosch  

SUMMARY

The 'morning ruminative state', with the at times accompanying flash phenomenon occurs during analysis, generally related to the transference. It represents one of the many derivatives of unresolved attempts at dealing with psychic strivings and generally follows dreams which similarly reflect those strivings which later are carried over to the semi-awakened state and are reflected in the morning ruminative state in a different form.

The flash signals a shift from the night-time attempts at resolving psychic conflicts to the morning ruminative state which is a continuation of these attempts. The flash sensation reflects sudden shifts in cathexis which facilitate the attempts at dealing with psychic conflicts, and may be experienced internally and externally in many forms.

The understanding of the process which brings into being the morning ruminative state as well as its content, sheds light on the patient's modes of dealing with psychic strivings.

[[RunningHead]]

Self and Object in Countertransference

Michael Moeller

Michael Moeller

Michael Lukas Moeller
Centre for Psychosomatic Medicine of the University
Friedrichstrasse 28
6300 Giessen

Michael Moeller  

1. ON THE DEFINITION OF COUNTERTRANSFERENCE

Countertransference, as we know, first came to notice as a disturbance in psychoanalytical treatment (Freud, 1910). Forty years later that typical change in attitudes towards countertransference took place which had already characterized the development of the concepts of 'resistance' and 'transference'. What had been an obstacle was now an effective instrument of therapy. Paula Heimann (1950) understood by countertransference all the emotions that arise in the analyst from unconscious depths as a reaction to the patient. Thus the unconscious becomes the key to understanding.

Although it is only fairly recently that countertransference has come in for increased attention, the extension of the concept has led to a confusing mass of definitions of varying character (cf. Orr, 1954).

The important positions can be classified in the following way: (1) there is a totalistic conception of countertransference: all feelings and attitudes of the doctor towards the patient are considered countertransference; (2) the so-called classical conception interprets countertransference as the unconscious reaction of the analyst to the transference of the patient (to this belong neurotic and non-neurotic reactions on the part of the analyst); and (3) the position of those who see in countertransference the complement or counterpart of the transference of the patient, i.e. a transference of the analyst's own infantile feelings to the patient.

In previous literature, then, the attitude of the analyst has in the main been classified on the basis of our criteria: normal v. neurotic, reactive v. general (i.e. feelings towards people in general).

The essential problem seems to me to consist in the fact that on the one hand countertransference is conceived as an appropriate and therapeutically important emotional reaction to the discovery of the patient's transference, and on the other hand as an inappropriate emotional reaction, distorting therapy and tending rather to obstruct the uncovering of the transference relationship.

[[RunningHead]]

Two Cases of Oedipal Conflict Beginning at Eighteen Months

Lisbeth Sachs

Lisbeth Sachs

Lisbeth J. Sachs
10 Plaza Street
Brooklyn, N.Y. 11238

Lisbeth Sachs  

Castration anxiety has been observed at an early stage in the infant's development. Freud (1909) wrote:

It has been urged that every time his mother's breast is withdrawn from a baby he is bound to feel it as castration (that is to say, as the loss of what he regards as an important part of his own body); that, further, he cannot fail to be similarly affected by the regular loss of his faeces; and, finally, that the act of birth itself (consisting as it does in the separation of the child from his mother, with whom he has hitherto been united) is the prototype of all castration. … The term 'castration complex' ought to be confined to those excitations and effects which are bound up with the loss of the penis (p. 8 fn.).

Several authors have observed early castration anxiety, though none of them links it to the oedipal conflict.

Loewenstein (1950) cites the case of a ten-months-old who wants to be reassured that the penis stays with the body and is not lost as a 'typical' phenomenon, but that only 'at a later stage, during the phallic phase of development, the boy experiences the impact of the castration complex …'. Greenacre (1968) postulates that towards the end of the second year there is 'some enhancement of genital activity' and links it to 'the increasing maturation of the body sphincters'. Glover (1945) writes: 'I have myself postulated primitive genital (nuclear) formations in early infancy, but that is not the same thing as postulating an active oedipus situation'.

Fenichel (1931), (1945) states that the analysis of adults contradicts the appearance of an early oedipal conflict. Zetzel (1956) takes a viewpoint similar to that of the above authors. Roiphe & Galenson (1972), in discussing 'Early Genital Activity and the Castration Complex', describe a girl who at 15 months showed 'pervasive curiosity about the genitals of other children.' They state that 'as far as can be determined, such early sexual development is free of any oedipal content.'

Melanie Klein (1928), (1945) on the other hand, placed 'the early stages of the Oedipus conflict … roughly from the middle of the first year to the third year of the child's life … It has been my invariable experience, for instance, that the night-terrors and phobias of small children are already due to the presence of an Oedipal conflict.

John Padel

John Padel  

Arthur Geoffrey Thompson, who died suddenly on 26 October 1976, was born of Quaker stock in Co. Wexford on 6 August 1905. After a Quaker preparatory school in Waterford he went to Portora Royal School, Enniskillen, in Ulster. He qualified in medicine at Trinity College, Dublin, in 1928. (His brother Alan, a year younger, pursued the same course one year behind him, stayed in Dublin and became Senior Physician at Richmond Hospital, Professor of Medicine at the Royal College of Surgeons and President of the Royal College of Physicians of Ireland in 1967; he died in 1974.) After qualification Geoffrey became Rockefeller Research Fellow in biochemistry, working in London (the Brompton and Barts) and in Paris. He returned to Dublin in 1930, became Physician at Bagot St Hospital; and his successful career made him the youngest (until then) Member of the Association of Physicians and FRCP(I).

In 1934 he renounced his brilliant prospects in Ireland and moved to London in order to train in psychoanalysis, holding psychiatric appointments at Bethlem and Maudsley Hospitals. Next year he began his career at The Tavistock Clinic under J. R. Rees. H. V. Dicks was a colleague there.

In the war he became a Major in the RAMC and worked for the WOSB. Then in 1946 he resumed psychiatric practice both privately and at the Tavistock, becoming a National Health Service employee at its inception. He stayed at the Tavistock Clinic till his retirement in 1970 and continued till his death to serve the Institute of Marital Studies, housed in the same building. He presided at the weekly clinical meetings of the IMS for 20 years and thereby exerted a persuasive influence on numerous staff. Literally hundreds of probation officers who took the annual course run by the IMS in conjunction with the Home Office were his pupils. Along with J. D. Sutherland and the Balints he taught junior colleagues how to recognize and facilitate the 'interactive processes' both within individuals and between them. His introductory chapter in 'Marriage: Studies in Emotional Conflict and Growth' (ed. L. Pincus, 1960) remains a seminal paper.

[[RunningHead]]

The Effect of Interpretation in Psychoanalytic Treatment in the Light of an Integrated Model of Internal Objects

Eliezer Ilan

Eliezer Ilan

Eliezer Ilan
27 Harakevet Street
Jerusalem

Eliezer Ilan  

I

The problem of therapeutic change in psychoanalysis continues to attract the attention of analysts in spite of the many papers written on this subject. Freud (1937) expresses some resentment towards the analysts who deal with it. He writes: 'In this field the interest of analysts seems to me to be quite wrongly directed. Instead of inquiry into how a cure by analysis comes about (a matter which I think has been sufficiently elucidated) the question should be asked of what are the obstacles that stand in the way of such a cure' (p. 221). Freud thought this subject more or less solved by what he had written many years before. In fact he dealt with the problem of the curative factors from the very beginning when he developed the psychoanalytic technique of treatment from its origins in the method of catharsis during hypnosis. In one of his earliest papers Freud (1904) mentions the two curative factors in the cathartic method, namely the recovery of memories during hypnosis which were inaccessible in the waking state and the strong expression of affect which these memories were loaded with. With the psychoanalytic treatment Freud discovered a method to reach these inaccessible memories and—as he later realized—also fantasies, without hypnosis, through the analyst's interpretation of the patient's free associations. The explanation of the curative effect by abreaction of 'strangulated' affects attached to unconscious memories seemed now far too simple and inadequate for the complicated processes that occurred in the psychoanalytic treatment. But the two factors in the cathartic method: the intrusion of unconscious ideation into the cognitive field and, simultaneously, a change in the realm of affects and emotions remained the two poles in the theory of the curative factors in psychoanalytic treatment. The link between these poles in the process of psychoanalytic treatment became a controversial issue in the psychoanalytic literature. Interpretation as the 'supreme agent' (Bibring, 1954) in psychoanalytic treatment had to effect changes in both areas. Freud tried to explain how this rational agent could change the cognitive and the emotional field and even the quality of the drives. He dealt with this problem mainly in terms of the prestructural period in his technical papers and in the last chapters of his 'Introductory Lectures on Psycho-Analysis' (Freud, 1917). He contends that interpretation of unconscious content could be accepted by the patient only in a state of positive affective attachment to the analyst, e.g. in sublimated, positive transference, and he points out three reasons as to why the patient is able to relinquish his symptoms in psychoanalytic treatment. First, his conscious ego is broadened, thus enabling him to understand and influence decisions and actions previously beyond his control, these being in the realm of the unconscious. Secondly, during the process of treatment, as the patient accepts the 'instructions' of the analyst, there develops a greater tolerance in the ego towards drives; and thirdly, a change in the quality of the drives takes place. Drives can now be sublimated as soon as they are made conscious.

[[RunningHead]]

Problems of Management in the Analysis of a Hallucinating Hysteric

Harold Stewart

Harold Stewart

Harold Stewart
16 Ferncroft Avenue
London NW3

Harold Stewart  

It is not often that one has the opportunity of analysing a patient who fights with a 'ghost' during the course of treatment and so I thought the case merited clinical discussion. I shall have little to say about the theoretical aspects of hallucinatory phenomena, but there are grounds for a discussion about various parameters that I used during the analysis which I think were necessary to sustain the analysis and bring it to a fairly successful conclusion.

Elizabeth Zetzel in her paper on 'The So-called Good Hysteric' (1968) which mainly concerns women patients, made a useful classification of hysterical patients into four grades according to their psychopathology and prospects of analysability. My patient would, I think, fall somewhere between her third and fourth grades and I shall briefly delineate what these grades represent. To quote:

Third, there are women with an underlying depressive character structure who frequently manifest hysterical symptomatology to a degree which disguises their deeper pathology. Fourth, there are women whose manifest hysterical symptomatology proves to be pseudo-oedipal and pseudo-genital. Such patients seldom meet the most important criteria for analysability.

She describes the fourth group as follows:

However, while their symptoms may present a façade which looks genital, they prove in treatment to be incapable of recognizing a genuine triangular situation. For them, as for Oedipus himself, the parent of the same sex has not remained a real object in any meaningful way. Such patients all too readily express intense sexualized transference phantasies. They tend, however, to regard such phantasies as potential areas of realistic gratification. They are genuinely incapable of the meaningful distinction between external and internal reality which is a pre-requisite for the establishment of a therapeutic alliance and the emergence of an analysable transference neurosis.

My patient was not quite as ill as this and was, perhaps also not a typical hysteric since severe depressions were one of her presenting symptoms. The discussion at the Paris Congress (Laplanche, 1974) showed the difficulties in defining the hysterical character, when compared say to the obsessional, and I do not wish to enter into that discussion here. I will, therefore, arbitrarily label her as a hysterical depressive character and proceed to describe her and the relevant aspects of the analysis which has lasted now for seven years.

responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Conceptions of Affect

André Green

André Green

André Green
6 rue du Val-de-Grâce
Paris V

André Green  

SUMMARY

By way of summary, it can be said that Freud's study of affect started from a point of view founded on coherent symptomatic groupings of neurotic symptoms, as a criterion for a discriminating study of the different vicissitudes of representations and affect, in the realms of the Conscious and Unconscious. As his work unfolded and his clinical experience grew, he progressively came to reverse the balance, in his final drive theory and the second topographical model, in favour of affects in relation to representations, as sell as extending the role of anxiety and differentiating aspects of it. Above all, he began to think of the drive as more and more independent

152

of the idea of content. If it is true that he came to recognize the value of the notion of quality (in pleasurable and unpleasurable states) late in the day; the economic point of view, on the other hand, was to be constantly recalled, so that its importance should not be neglected. Finally, his last period of work was to be marked by an insistence on the primitive distress of the young child, on the general psychological immaturity of the ego at birth and on a shift of emphasis towards the primary object, which, in the first part of his work, was conceived primarily as an object of pleasure (and a force for repression) and subsequently became an object of survival, for building the ego up against the disorganizing power of the drives when it failed against them. Nevertheless, Freud remains faithful to the end to the opposition of representation and affect.

In the second period, a new way of seeing the problems developed. The development of categories of syndromes no longer constituted the pertinent boundaries for evaluating psychic functioning. If allusion is still made to them, it is in order to stress the differences between the classical neuroses and clinical aspects of modern practice, where differentiated groups of syndromes carry less interest than types of functioning which are no longer distinguished in nosographical terms but according to general types of structure (character neuroses, limited states, depression, psychotic or psychosomatic structures, etc.). Whilst reference continues to be made to the substance of the second topographical model, clinical formulations are more often made in terms of object relations and defence mechanisms. The distinction between affect and representations gave way to another distinction, implicitly considered to be more in accord with the analytic situation—that between cathexis and unconscious fantasy, whilst one theme emerged predominant: the study of the prehistory of the mother–child relationship, examined from different angles and in different ways, but with affective factors to the fore.

Finally, the concept of the self emerged from various directions, having been, so to speak, suppressed by the object-relations approach. Similarly, without denying its specificity, the function of fantasy is connoted by that of experience. Both are found again in the setting of communication between analyst and patient and inside each of them, which obliges us to consider the value, function, effects and specific modes of the affective exchanges at the core of mental functioning, and of the changes which appear desirable in the context of transformations expected in analyses. But then the question arises about the different sorts of meaning carried in the material, the different means of transmitting them, and the type of response which they induce or call for in the analyst. All of which comes down to three questions: What is the most appropriate form of response to the patient's affective communication, in order to secure better intrapsychic communication in him? What sort of logic is implied by this type of exchange on both sides? What is the connexion between affect in this relationship and what might be called affective logic?

responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

On Working with 'Unwanted' Patients

Michael Eigen

Michael Eigen

Michael Eigen
273 W. 90th Street
New York N.Y. 10024

Michael Eigen  

The 'unwanted' patient often goes through similar trials in trying to find a therapist who can and will work with him as he does in trying to find people who are able to sustain benign interactions with him in his daily life. These patients tend to drive therapists away as they do people in general. They generally present severe narcissistic character deformations which are more than usually exasperating or repulsive. They may oscillate between an over-cloying and obnoxiously negativistic manner in apparently endless repetition of extreme forms of hostile dependence. They can appear needy and demanding yet seem to present intractable resistances if one tries to help them. In some instances they may appear snake-like and cynically chilling. If they are vegetative, they are also wilful and proud, even when in seemingly masochistic and silent ways. They are very sensitive to slights but seem to have long ago accepted that the best they can do is just manage to get through things. They carry a hope tinged with resentment, made heavy with an accusing sense of deprivation and self-pity. They seek relief from pain but have a high tolerance for feeling that things will never change and nothing good can happen to them. However, all this despair seems part of an atmosphere of muted want and rage. Their identity is formed by a chronic sense of injury together with a primitive union with the phallic mother—male or female—who injured them. Thus one moment they may enact the role of helpless victim and then become a terrifying active mass, impulsive, tyrannical or biting. Their personality deformations, further, frequently express themselves in physically unappealing ways, such as unkempt obesity or severely distorting facial and postural rigidities. In these cases the actual body has become closely identified with and formed by a negative self image.

In general, these patients tend to evoke some sense of distaste in the therapists who try to treat them. Their basically constricted ways of behaving seem to set off and accentuate their psychophysical distortions and tend to have a constricting-distorting effect on others, including the therapist. Their frequent use of denial and externalization may habitually frustrate the therapist who is primarily interested in verbal reports of inner experience. The therapist will find it difficult not to experience some form of disgust or anger as treatment develops. He may try to defend himself by becoming sleepy or bored or subtly fight to maintain his sense of boundaries in a paedagogical way. However, he cannot help these patients by remaining emotionally uninvolved. As in work with children and psychotics, suitable cognitive-affective responses are required for maximal healing to occur. The problem facing the therapist is how to provide the specific emotional responses which will facilitate the patient's growth when the instinctive responses the patient evokes are largely negative. The patient who appears gross, dull, chilling or otherwise 'impossible' and yet seeks genuine help (in however magical a way) presents a radical challenge to the well-meaning practitioner's image of himself and therapy.

[[RunningHead]]

The Psychoanalytic Concept of Schizophrenia: I. the Dilemma

James Grotstein

James Grotstein

James S. Grotstein
9777 Wilshire Boulevard
Beverly Hills, Cal. 90212

James Grotstein  

SUMMARY

In Part I of this paper I have addressed my attention to what I believe are weaknesses in current psychoanalytic theory in so far as it is: (a) based on an instinctual theory emphasizing primacy of discharge rather than on the primacy of object relations; and (b) based on the fundamental importance of the infantile neurosis which, I hope I have demonstrated, is neither infantile nor neurotic. Instead, I have opted for a broader theory, which Klein and Fairbairn have long ago adumbrated, based upon the premise of an infantile psychology from the beginning of extrauterine life, functioning on a dual track system of both differentiation (and thus perception and cognition, though rudimentary) and dedifferentiation (the phantasied reunion with the object to deny the awareness of separation).

Of particular importance in normal and pathological development is the evolution of internal objects and their ultimate fate, i.e. whether they are externalized or projected. The sense of personal identity seems to rest on identification with these objects and subsequent release from these identifications. Each period of development will be the ongoing serial of the self in struggle and in harmony with transformations of these internal objects. This mythic infrastructure is an incompletely told tale which has too long been divorced from the superstructure of ego psychology.

The aim of Part I of this presentation is to redevelop a psychoanalytic theory of narcissism or infantile psychosis as an organizing framework for all normal and pathological development, so that the narcissistic (or 'psychotic' core) of later entities can be better understood and so that schizophrenia in particular may have a uniform theory worthy of it.

Now that earliest infancy has been given a psychology, one that is populated by phantasied objects which are inherent residues and precursors to the awarenesses of external objects (pre-object objects, if you will), the importance of the developmental point of view can at last be harvested, especially in terms of psychoanalytic technique and understanding. The course of mythic personality progression would ontogenetically dictate that each successive stage of development unfolds as an assimilation of the previous stages and is directly and indirectly modified by them. Thus the order of priority in psychoanalytic technique would be directed to the deeper and earlier stages of development. Analysing an Oedipus complex initially would run the hazard of disallowing the optional evolution of a more nearly-complete Oedipus complex as a result of the therapeutic lysing of the adhesion which impeded and altered its evolution initially.

In Part II I shall try to avail myself of this scheme to account for the pathogenesis of schizophrenia in particular and psychosis in general.

[[RunningHead]]

Affects and the Psychoanalytic Situation

Adam Limentani

Adam Limentani

Adam Limentani
5 Upper Wimpole Street
London W1

Adam Limentani  

I

In announcing the main topic for the Jerusalem Congress, the Programme Committee noted that the problem concerning affects seemed appropriate as a focus for the discussions, since no two theories agree on it. The truth of this statement is affirmed by the most casual reappraisal of the literature as stated in a number of outstanding contributions such as those of Brierley (1951), Rapaport (1953), Rangell (1967) and Green (1973). Green's Le Discours Vivant, a very comprehensive work on the subject, has not attracted sufficient attention because it has appeared only in French. Papers written in more recent years have continued to highlight the discrepancy of approach to the problem in various parts of the world, due not entirely to parochialism on the part of some writers, but rather because of the disparities between theory, technique and clinical practice. The major areas of theoretical research are: (1) the drive discharge theory, (2) the debate on the existence of unconscious affects, (3) the relationship to their mental representations and fantasies, (4) the issue concerning the possibility of affects dissociated from the object, (5) the ego as the only seat of anxiety, (6) the problem of narcissistic and schizoid personality disturbances, and (7) the widespread calls for adjustments and modifications of the classical method in the treatment of borderline and narcissistic states, which has generated further complications in the matching of theory with practice. Although the task of reviewing the literature has been undertaken by André Green (this issue) the subject of the affects in the analytic situation remains vast. In this paper I shall not concentrate on any one aspect of the theory of the affects or of a specific clinical phenomenon, nor will I attempt to supply any answers. I shall, however, underline certain diagnostic features which are fundamental to an understanding of the state of malaise existing in the relationship between theory and our clinical daily work.

responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Clinical Observations on Resistance to the Transference

Samuel Lipton

Samuel Lipton

Samuel D. Lipton
25 East Washington Street
Chicago, Ill. 60602

Samuel Lipton  

The importance of resistance to the transference is so well known and so amply written about that it seems unlikely that anything worthwhile can still be written on the subject. Nevertheless, from the responses to the presentation of my data during the past decade, from the literature, and from the reports of patients who have had prior analysis, I have become convinced that certain resistances are not interpreted as frequently as they can be advantageously, and I think that it is quite possible that they are not recognized. Therefore it may be useful to document my clinical experience.

There are two forms of resistance to the transference which are ubiquitous. One is the form in which the self representation remains intact and the transference is alluded to by displacement. This form is more generally recognized and interpreted. I shall cite a few examples of it mainly to make my meaning clear. The second form is one in which the self representation is changed, typically by means of a transitory identification of the patient with the analyst. This form is the one that I think is less well recognized and I shall illustrate it more extensively.

The clinical material I am reporting is restricted to the concept I am describing. It concerns the most immediate, most superficial level of analysis. It concerns the recognition of the transference which is only the first step in its development and in its interpretation and resolution. What I am reporting is what occurs before interpretations of genesis can be begun in a meaningful way, that is in any way other than the intellectual hypotheses which can be derived from a history itself.

As an example of the form of resistance in which the self representation is intact, I cite an incident from the analysis of a young woman who was convinced at the time that I was unduly attracted to her and that I was therefore very restrained and punctiliously correct. One hour she reported that she had gone to the 'movies' and had seen a feature film which had offended her and a short subject which she had found amusing. The plot of the feature film was an arrangement made by a young couple to live together without having sexual relations, in order to determine if their interests were compatible before committing themselves to marriage. The man was sexually stimulated repeatedly, and his efforts to dissipate his sexual excitement by means of physical exercise were intended to be comical, but she found the theme offensive.

[[RunningHead]]

The Psychoanalytic Concept of Schizophrenia: II. Reconciliation

James Grotstein

James Grotstein

James S. Grotstein
9777 Wilshire Boulevard
Beverly Hills, Calif. 90212

James Grotstein  

SUMMARY

This presentation has attempted to create a confluence of the neurobehavioural and biological data and the psychoanalytic metapsychology of schizophrenia so as to allow for its ultimate conceptualization. In so doing, I have tried to show that the present psychoanalytic model which is based on instinctual drive theory and the centrality of the phallic Oedipus complex does not do justice to schizophrenia. I have tentatively hinted at a psychoanalytic theory which is based upon the infantile 'psychosis' as the progenitor of psychosis, narcissistic disorders and the neurosis, in order to enfranchise the infantile psychotic positions, and the narcissism under which they serve, as the most important determinants of personality development. An unresolved or bizzarely derived infantile psychosis eventuates in schizophrenia. Psychoanalysis needs to be a theory which can subtend such a concept. Furthermore, it needs a theoretical framework which is based upon the importance of internal objects which, more than drives, constitutes the scaffolding of the personality from the beginning to the end of life. In short, schizophrenia requires a psychoanalytic theory worthy of it. In return, this theory can clarify the narcissistic core of all the other disturbances, can bring normal personality development and its pathological vicissitudes into line with all psychopathology generally, in a manner which is more sophisticated and phenomenologically precise than has hitherto existed.

As a result of the application of this revised theory to schizophrenia, this presentation has tried to distinguish (a) schizophrenia from psychosis; and (b) reactive schizophrenia (psychotic regression) from true schizophrenia, in which an altered behavioural development has been going on from the beginning of life. The 'conflict' school and the 'ego defect' school are then reconciled by a unified theory of psychoanalysis which emphasizes the interrelationship between narcissism, infantile psychosis and infant development. Thus a quantitatively significant fixation in early infancy may confirm the 'ego defect' point of view, and a quantitatively serious regression could confirm the 'conflict' point of view. In short, both are correct; they erred largely in terms of their generalizations and in the mutual exclusiveness of their definitions.

A major yet less highlighted theme of this presentation is the importance of phantasy, not alone as a reconciling medium of communication in the internal world of the normal and the schizophrenic psyche prior to formal language structuralization, but also as a confluence for all the neurobehavioural and psychoanalytic tributaries of knowledge. Phantasy, as the mental representation of the human biological infrastructure, should occupy a prominent seat in the investigative and clinical researches into schizophrenia because of its unique centralizing and constellating propensity, and because it is the interface of the brain-mind continuum. It is no exaggeration to liken it to a Rosetta Stone to the hieroglyphic of preverbal communication in general and schizophrenic communication in particular.

Yet another aim of this monograph from an even more global perspective has been to introduce an updated, modified and ultimately unified psychoanalytic theory which can be reintroduced harmoniously with its behavioural science relatives to form a neurobehavioural entity in which the invariant clinical findings of schizophrenia and psychosis can be located and denoted holographically, that is, simultaneously, from all points of view, yet as manifestations of psyche most of all. Thus, the passive stimulus barrier, a neurodevelopmental construct, can be seen as the harmonious action of the limbic and reticular systems neurophysiologically, the normal action of the dopamine-serotonin-methyl transferase systems neurochemically, the precursor for a differentiating grid psycholinguistically, and the anlage for adhesive identification, omnipotence and primal repression psychically. It is the necessity for this kind of correlation that this paper has addressed itself to.

responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

The Pleasure Principle

Joseph Smith

Joseph Smith

Joseph H. Smith
4705 Locust Hill Court
Bethesda, Maryland

Joseph Smith  

SUMMARY

The pleasure principle is one way of conceptualizing the inevitability of directionality of behaviour. In radically intentionalized terms — as though pure intentionality were possible — regulation by the pleasure principle is such that one always, without exception, strives to do what he wants to do in the way he wants to do it and to the extent he wants to do it. This is not hedonism. It covers anything and everything that a human being could conceivably want to do from the relatively passive pre-intentional (prior, that is, to any possibility of wanting or willing) directionality of primitive function and holds through the highest degree of human autonomy. Of course the content of what one wants to do — which is quite a different thing from the empty, formal assumption that all behaviour is directional — can radically change depending upon a host of factors which might be relevant to a particular stage of development and a particular situation. Obstacles, for instance, not only interfere with one's doing what he wants to do, they also instigate modification of motive and thus differentiation of possible motives. When the infant learns that the hallucinated object of wish-fulfilment does not provide satisfaction; that is one step in differentiating inner and outer, which permits the content of his wishing to be directed toward the object in reality along the pathway of thought.

Freud confused the content of directionality with the formal assumption of directionality. He thus enunciated two pleasure principles, one capital and the other a kind of lower-case content version referring to the fact that the direction of behaviour is often away from pain and towards pleasure. Freud was aware that the two could not be compressed into one without radically redefining pain and pleasure from their ordinary meanings; he was aware, that is, that the lower-case pleasure principle did not hold universally. Conceptualization in terms of the lower-case pleasure principle required invocation of a reality principle when the situation changed. His acknowledgment that the reality principle was but a modification of the pleasure principle indicated his awareness that something universal — the Pleasure Principle — persisted through change.

[[RunningHead]]

Notes on the Signal Function of Hypochondriacal Anxiety

Robert Stolorow

Robert Stolorow

Robert D. Stolorow
Department of Psychology, Yeshiva University
55 Fifth Avenue
New York, N.Y. 10003

Robert Stolorow  

I recently (re)discovered two interesting passages in Freud's (1914) paper on narcissism, in which he speculates about a possible parallel between the development of neurotic anxiety in the transference neuroses and the occurrence of hypochondriacal anxiety in the narcissistic neuroses:

I will merely mention that from this point of view we may suspect that the relation of hypochondria to paraphrenia is similar to that of the other 'actual' neuroses to hysteria and obsessional neurosis: we may suspect, that is, that it is dependent on ego-libido just as the others are on object-libido, and that hypochondriacal anxiety is the counterpart, as coming from ego-libido, to neurotic anxiety. Further, since we are already familiar with the idea that the mechanism of falling ill and of the formation of symptoms in the transference neuroses—the path from introversion to regression—is to be linked to a damming-up of object-libido, we may come to closer quarters with the idea of a damming-up of ego-libido as well and may bring this idea into relation with the phenomena of hypochondria and paraphrenia (pp. 84–85).

The difference between paraphrenic affections and the transference neuroses appears to me to lie in the circumstance that, in the former, the libido that is liberated by frustration does not remain attached to objects in phantasy, but withdraws on to the ego. Megalomania would accordingly correspond to the psychical mastering of this latter amount of libido, and would thus be the counterpart of the introversion on to phantasies that is found in the transference neuroses; a failure of this psychical function gives rise to the hypochondria of paraphrenia and this is homologous to the anxiety of the transference neuroses (p. 86).

Essentially, Freud argues here that hypochondriacal anxiety results from the damming-up of narcissistic libido, just as neurotic anxiety derives from the damming-up of object libido. His argument rests on his early economic concept of anxiety as the automatic somatic consequence of the repression of libidinal excitation. We know, of course, that following his creation of the structural theory, he revised his conception of anxiety and viewed it as an affect generated by the ego to signal anticipated intrapsychic danger and the need for defence (Freud, 1926).

[[RunningHead]]

The Golden Fantasy: A Regressive Reaction to Separation Anxiety

Sydney Smith

Sydney Smith

Sydney Smith
The Menninger Foundation
P. O. Box 829
Topeka, Kansas 66601

Sydney Smith  

The analysis of fantasy is a prominent aspect of the work of psychoanalytic treatment. For historical reasons dream interpretation has received the lion's share of our professional interest in fantasy, but every analyst is aware that daydreams, reveries, conscious imagery and flights of fancy can provide significant grist for the treatment mill. The expectation is that bringing into focus the unanalysed meanings, the wishful musings embedded in fantasies of whatever nature will further strengthen the ego's hold on reality. But in the course of analysis with many patients a specific hidden fantasy eventually unfolds which not only has a fixed content noteworthy for its similarity from patient to patient, but which becomes the basis of a significant resistance to treatment.

The fantasy is a simple and familiar one: it is the wish to have all of one's needs met in a relationship hallowed by perfection. Two invariable features of this fantasy make it a thing apart both in its effect upon the patient's life and in its influence on the analysis. The first aspect has to do with the patient's position in respect to the fantasy: it is always passive, always tied to the conviction that somewhere in that great, unbounded expanse called the world is a person capable of fully meeting one's needs. The wish is to be cared for so completely that no demand will be made on the patient except his capacity for passively taking in. The second aspect, a corollary of the first, is the subjective experience of the patient that this fantasy touches on the deepest issues of one's life and that indeed one's very survival may depend upon its preservation. To maintain the fantasy intact, to search endlessly for its fulfilment in every relationship becomes the patient's raison d'être. To give up the fantasy is to give up everything, to lose the primary source of comfort (the idealized object), even one's sense of meaning. It is as if the fantasy provided a self-definition: without it there is no existence and the world becomes a place without hope. One can sense the black and white, all-or-nothing quality of this formula, as if on the other side of the ideal there lurked some horror. If one cannot maintain what is good, then one is left only with what is bad; the danger is that the ideal object can become a persecutory one.

[[RunningHead]]

Psychotherapy and Psychoanalysis: Theory–Practice–Research

By Robert S. Wallerstein. New York: International Universities Press. 1975. Pp. 475.

P. Kuiper

P. Kuiper

P. Kuiper  

The contents of Dr Wallerstein's book are so rich that in a review such as this no attempt can be made to summarize its various chapters. Several points, however, which drew my attention, will be discussed.

In the first chapter, it is made clear that the behaviour and cognitive functions of a patient with an organic brain disturbance is the result not only of his cerebral dysfunction but also of his regression as an emotional reaction to his loss of functioning.

During psychoanalytic treatment, the image that the patient acquires of the analyst is determined by the transference neurosis. Sometimes delusions can originate if the reality-testing function is lost and the patient is no longer capable of experiencing his own feelings as a transference phenomenon. This is discussed in chapter II, 'Reconstruction and Mastery in the Transference Psychosis.' It is striking again and again to see how strongly convinced of their judgement patients can be when influenced by a transference neurosis. The intensity and duration of the symptoms will determine whether they are psychotic or neurotic. Everyone—certainly everyone influenced by strong transference feelings—can make faulty interpretations of reality. The inability to correct this interpretation implies the development of a transference psychosis.

Further, the contents of this book can be classified in general as: (1) psychotherapy and the relation of psychotherapy to psychoanalysis; (2) research in psychotherapy; and (3) several topics with strong social implications. In the chapters concerning psychotherapy and the relation of psychotherapy and psychoanalysis, it is clearly stated that these two techniques must not be compared in one but, rather, in several aspects. Dr Wallerstein disputes the idea that the contrast psychoanalysis–psychotherapy coincides with the contrast psychoanalysis–suggestion. Other approaches must be considered as well: e.g. suggestion, abreaction, clarification, interpretation, giving advice. Concerning the contrast expressive–supportive, several nuances are suggested. An interpretation can give the best and most effective support. The problem, as suggested by Frieda Fromm-Reichmann, to what extent psychotherapeutic techniques require a theory other than the psychoanalytic one, is of great importance. The author believes that dynamic psychotherapy uses the same scientific system as psychoanalysis. It is certainly unnecessary to attribute a separate theory to dynamic psychotherapy. The question: 'Is a psychoanalytical theory possible for the non-analytic therapies?' is also discussed. In the opinion of the reviewer, one can only consider learning theory as a theory which is complemented by psychoanalysis. I have argued elsewhere that learning theory is more suited to fit in the framework of psychoanalytic theory than the other way around.

[[RunningHead]]

The Advantages of Freud's Technique as Shown in his Analysis of the Rat Man

Samuel Lipton

Samuel Lipton

Samuel D. Lipton
25 East Washington Street
Chicago, Ill. 60602

Samuel Lipton  

While psychoanalytic technique is often considered uniform or standardized, there are actually many areas of disagreement. These disagreements are more concealed or glossed over than revealed in the literature, but I assume that other analysts, like me, have learned about them in discussions with colleagues and from the reports of patients who have been treated by other analysts. The fact that these disagreements exist and the complicating fact that they are not documented plainly makes it impossible to reach general conclusions about technique. In my opinion what is referred to as standard technique may be considered a trend, perhaps a dominant trend, and in what I designate as modern technique I mean no more than that. It is only for clarity in exposition that henceforward I shall refer to Freud's technique and modern techniques as if there were a sharp, uniform distinction. I shall attempt to distinguish the two techniques and to point out the advantages of Freud's technique over modern technique.

While the issue I am discussing is broader than can be subsumed under the discussion of a single case, I shall base an important part of my argument on Freud's technique in his analysis of the Rat Man (Freud, 1909) mainly because by that means I can document a number of points. I shall defend the argument that Freud's technique in that case was his definitive technique and point out how it differs from modern technique. Then I shall describe the difference further with information I have learned in discussions with colleagues which is not in the literature. I shall defend the argument that an important difference, and perhaps the important difference, between Freud's technique and modern technique is a redefinition of the concept of technique, a redefinition which broadens the concept beyond the limits of Freud's definition. Finally, I shall argue that despite the idea that this redefinition of technique is considered an advance, it is in fact disadvantageous.

I

I turn now to the case of the Rat Man. Since every analyst is familiar with the case, it is unnecessary to review it in detail and I shall confine myself to citing certain points which are relevant to my thesis.

The Borderline Ego and the Working Alliance: Indications for Family and Individual Treatment in Adolescence

Edward Shapiro , Roger Shapiro , John Zinner and David Berkowitz

David Berkowitz

Edward R. Shapiro
Director, Adolescent and Family Treatment and Study Center, McLean Hospital
Belmont, Mass. 02178
Roger L. Shapiro
WASHINGTON, D.C.;
John Zinner
WASHINGTON, D.C.
David A. Berkowitz
BOSTON

David Berkowitz  

SUMMARY

The borderline patient is characterized by serious ego weakness with a pathological use of splitting as a defence. During his adolescence, the patient's difficulty in distinguishing the therapist from his projections of internalized (split) objects can be severely aggravated by concurrent regressive family interaction which repeatedly confirms these projections. Because of this family regression, there may be increased difficulty in sustaining a stable working alliance with the use of individual therapy alone despite the use of technical modifications (decreased frequency of sessions, limit-setting, manipulation, etc.)

The authors suggest that in the treatment of borderline adolescents, the presence of the individual therapist in concurrent family therapy can clarify the reality of the family's regressed behaviour for patient and therapist with the aid of the therapist's observing ego. In the individual transference, when the patient's regression evokes a re-enactment of these interactions with the therapist, this shared experience can be called upon to supplement and support the working alliance. The authors provide parallel excerpts from family and individual treatment to illustrate the use of this concurrent treatment approach.
